title: Chapter 14: The Pact
content: The basement door opened on silence.

No scratching this time, no feral stillness pressed against the far wall. Bertha sat at the table as though she'd been waiting — not with the coiled tension of a trapped animal, but with something quieter. Patience, maybe. Or the absence of hope that had finally curdled into something more useful.

Jane closed the door behind her and crossed to the table. The overhead bulb threw its usual anemic light across the concrete, turning everything the color of old bone. She placed the small glassine envelope on the table between them and sat down.
Bertha looked at the envelope. Then at Jane. Then back at the envelope.

She picked it up with careful fingers, slid the flap open, and drew out the thread. Red. Coarsely spun, uneven in gauge, beautiful in the way that only handmade things could be — each imperfection a record of the hand that made it. Bertha rolled the thread between her thumb and forefinger. She brought it close to her face, and her nostrils flared slightly, reading the scent.
Then she smiled.

It transformed her entirely. Not into someone younger or softer — into someone real. The angular planes of her face rearranged themselves around genuine feeling, and Jane saw, for one disorienting moment, the woman Bertha must have been before Thornfield took its toll. Before Edward took his toll.
"You recognized my thread," Bertha said.
"I recognized the dye," Jane replied. "Madder root. The undertone's unmistakable — that warm brown beneath the red. You can't fake it with synthetics. You made this yourself."
Bertha's fingers kept rolling the thread, feeling its texture against her skin like a musician testing a string. "The things you have to invent when they take your tools away."

The words hung in the basement air, and Jane let them. She didn't fill the silence with sympathy or shock. Bertha didn't need either. She needed the thread to be seen for what it was: proof that someone had been listening, even when the world believed her voice had gone out.

For a long moment, neither of them spoke. The basement breathed around them — the faint drone of ventilation, the settle of stone, the distant hum of Thornfield's climate systems keeping the archive above at precisely nineteen degrees Celsius and sixty percent humidity. Conditions perfect for fabric. Not for people.
Jane looked at Bertha. Bertha looked at Jane.
And something passed between them that had nothing to do with Edward Rochester.

It wasn't solidarity born of shared suffering — their suffering wasn't the same, and Jane had enough honesty to know it. It wasn't the bitter kinship of women wronged by the same man, because that framing made Edward the center of a story he didn't deserve to anchor. What moved between them was simpler and rarer: recognition. Two women who had seen the same trap from opposite ends and understood, without needing to explain, exactly how it worked.

No rivalry. No jealousy. None of the territorial possessiveness that the world expected women to perform over a man who was, by any objective measure, a monster. Jane felt no impulse to claim priority, to say *I was with him first* or *he hurt me more.* The very idea was obscene. You didn't compete for standing in a house fire. You got out, and you pulled the other person with you.
Bertha extended her hand.

Not a handshake — Jane saw that immediately. The fingers were angled wrong for a shake, the palm offered at a different tilt. A seam. The old garment-worker's greeting: right hands overlapping, fingers pressing the gap between each other's knuckles. The grip that said *I know your craft. I trust your hands.*

Jane's grandmother had taught her that greeting in the workroom above the shop in Gateshead, when Jane was small enough to sit on the cutting table with her legs dangling. She hadn't seen it performed in years. But her body remembered. She reached out and returned it — her right hand settling against Bertha's, fingers curling into the spaces between knuckles, the pressure firm and specific. The grip lasted three seconds. It said everything.
The pact was sealed without a word.

Bertha released her hand and leaned back. The smile had faded, but the warmth it left behind hadn't. Her expression settled into something focused, sharp — the look of a woman who had been waiting six years for a conversation worth having.
"All right," she said. "Here's how we do it."
Jane would go back up.

That was the first principle. She would return to her room, to her post in the archive, to the careful performance of the archivist who was grateful for her position and slowly, subtly, inexorably entranced by the master of Thornfield. She would let Edward draw her deeper — into his confidence, his plans, his bed if that was what it took, though the thought turned her stomach like a swallowed coin. The closer she got, the more access she would have. To his schedule. To his correspondence. To the financial records that lived on his private server and the digital infrastructure that held Thornfield's entire operation together like invisible thread.

"I can get to his systems," Jane said. "He's already given me archive access. It's a small step from cataloguing garments to cataloguing data. He wants me organized. He wants me useful. I'll be both."
Bertha nodded slowly, her fingers still rolling the red thread like a rosary.

"And I'll use this," she said. She tilted her head toward the ceiling — toward the floors above, where Thornfield's digital nervous system ran through walls and conduits. "The wireless signal down here is broken. Edward thinks it's a dead zone. He's half right — the signal can't reach out. But it can reach up. I designed Thornfield's digital archive six years ago. Every database, every cataloguing protocol, every backup routine. I wrote the architecture before Edward decided I was too unstable to touch a keyboard."
Her voice was steady, but Jane heard the granite beneath it — the compressed fury of a woman who had built a house and then been locked in its basement.

"He changed the passwords," Bertha continued. "He migrated the servers. He thinks he locked me out. But he didn't know what I built. There are back doors in that system he's never found. Maintenance tunnels in the code. I can see everything — financials, correspondence, the raw design files before he strips my name off them. I can move through his data like a ghost."
Her eyes met Jane's. "He thinks he controls the narrative. But I wrote the code that stores it."
Jane felt a current run through her — not quite joy, not quite hope, but something adjacent to both. The thrill of impossible odds becoming merely difficult ones. She leaned forward.
"We need a way to communicate. If we're both operating inside his system, we need channels he can't read."
"I know," Bertha said. "That's the easy part."
The communication protocols came together in forty minutes, built from the raw materials they had: fabric and code. Two languages. Two channels. Both invisible to anyone who didn't know how to look.

Jane would leave messages in the archive. Physical messages, hidden in the seam allowances of specific garments — just as Bertha had done, but using a different code. Jane's system would be based on stitch direction: right-slanting for *proceed*, left-slanting for *wait*, paired knots for *urgent*. The garments themselves would serve as timestamps — the piece closest to the archive's entrance for the most recent message, the deeper pieces for older communications. A filing system made of silk and cotton.
"I'll mark the ones I've encoded with a double notch on the label," Jane said. "It'll look like a tagging error. Nobody questions a librarian's quirks."

Bertha almost smiled again. "And I'll respond through the digital system. Automated maintenance logs — server updates, backup confirmations, routine diagnostics. I'll bury the messages in the metadata. Timestamps, file sizes, hex strings in the header fields. Edward doesn't read his own maintenance reports. Nobody does. If he intercepts our mail, he'll see server updates. Boring. Invisible."

"What about Grace?" Jane asked. The name sat between them like a stone. Grace Poole, the watcher. The woman who brought Bertha her meals and checked the locks and asked no questions that Edward hadn't authorized. "She sees everything that moves in this house."

Bertha's expression didn't change, but something shifted behind her eyes — a calculation, cold and precise. "Grace sees what she's paid to see. She doesn't look harder than that. She hasn't in six years."

Jane absorbed this. It wasn't reassurance. It was intelligence — a data point about an asset who might be neutral or might be hostile, depending on variables they couldn't yet calculate. She filed it alongside everything else.
"And the target?" Jane asked. "We can plan channels forever, but we need to know where we're aiming."
Bertha set the thread down on the table. She laced her fingers together and leaned forward.
"Paris Fashion Week."
The words landed with the weight of something inevitable. Jane felt her pulse shift.

"Thornfield's closing show," Bertha continued. "The biggest fashion event of the season. Global livestream — four hundred million viewers. Every buyer, every editor, every influencer with a ring light and an agenda, all watching. It's also Edward's IPO announcement. He'll stand on that runway and tell the world that Thornfield is going public. That his vision is worth two billion euros. That he built this empire alone."
Her voice dropped half a register.

"That's the moment. The most public he'll ever be. The most exposed. The one minute when he can't hide behind lawyers, can't lock doors, can't spin the narrative backstage. Four hundred million people watching, and none of them will be looking away."
Jane's mind was already running ahead, sketching the shape of an operation she couldn't yet see in full. The scale of it was terrifying. The possibility was intoxicating.
"That's when we show them," Bertha said. "Not in a boardroom. Not in a courtroom. On his runway. In front of the whole world."

The basement hummed. Somewhere above them, Thornfield's climate system cycled on, pushing conditioned air through ducts that Bertha had mapped in her mind a thousand times, routing through passages she had built and Edward had forgotten she'd built.
"How long do we have?" Jane asked.
"The show is in eleven weeks. That's enough time if we move fast and don't make mistakes."

Eleven weeks. Jane turned the number over in her mind. Eleven weeks to infiltrate Edward's inner circle, access his financial records, map his vulnerabilities, and coordinate a strike that would play out on the most visible stage in the fashion industry — all while maintaining the cover of a compliant archivist and relying on a communication system made of stitch codes and fake server logs.
It was impossible. It was the only plan that mattered.

Jane rose from the table. The basement air clung to her skin — cool, slightly damp, carrying the mineral scent of stone that had been underground for centuries. She had been here for less than an hour, but the world above felt distant, a country she would have to re-enter as someone she was choosing, deliberately, to pretend to be.
She crossed to the door. Her hand found the handle. And then she stopped.
She turned back.
Bertha was still at the table, the red thread coiled in her palm like a small, stubborn pulse.
"Bertha."
The other woman looked up.
"How did you know?" Jane asked. "That I would find the codes. The ones in the seam allowances. You'd been encoding messages for years, and nobody had ever found them. How did you know I would?"
Bertha was quiet for a long time. The basement filled with the small sounds of two women breathing, the whisper of the ventilation, the almost inaudible tick of the pipes.
"I didn't," Bertha said finally.
Jane held her gaze. "Then why keep making them?"

Another silence. Longer this time. Bertha looked down at the thread in her hand — the rough, handspun fiber, imperfect and indelible, dyed with a root that had been staining cloth red for five thousand years. She touched it the way Jane had seen women touch things they were afraid to need: gently, as if pressure might make it disappear.
"Because the alternative was silence." Her voice was barely above a whisper, but it filled the basement like a held breath. "And silence is the only death I'm not ready for."
Jane nodded. There was nothing to add. She pulled the door open and stepped into the corridor.

The hallway stretched ahead, lit by the same sickly bulbs, the same bare stone. Jane moved quickly, her footsteps soft against the concrete, her body remembering the route the way it had the last two nights — turn left at the junction, up the service stairs, through the door that opened onto the ground-floor gallery.
She was halfway to the junction when she heard it.

A creak. Above her. Not the settling of old wood — she knew that sound intimately now, had catalogued every groan and sigh that Thornfield's bones made in the dark. This was different. A single, deliberate pressure. A foot on a floorboard in the corridor directly overhead, stepping from stillness into motion.
Jane froze.
She pressed herself against the wall and tilted her head, listening. The ventilation hummed. The pipes ticked. The floorboard did not creak again.

Someone had been there. In the corridor above. Close enough to the basement door — close enough to the seams in the old stone that carried sound like a whisper through a keyhole — to have heard voices. Two voices. Low, but unmistakably human.

Jane held her breath and counted to twenty. No second creak. No retreating footsteps. Whoever it was had gone still, or had already moved away on the carpeted sections of the upper floor where footsteps made no sound.

She straightened. She walked the rest of the corridor without rushing — running would make noise, and noise would tell the listener that she knew. She climbed the service stairs, passed through the gallery door, and emerged into the ground-floor hallway of Thornfield as though she had merely been for a restless walk in the lower levels, unable to sleep.
The hall was empty. The château was quiet. The archive doors stood closed, and the faint green light of the security panel blinked its steady, indifferent rhythm.
Jane made it to her room. She closed the door. She leaned against it and pressed her palms flat against the wood.
A witness. Someone had heard them.
The question circled her like a hawk over open ground: friend or enemy?
In a house built on secrets, the difference could be measured in minutes. And Jane had no way of knowing which way those minutes would fall.
